title: feat: XDG base directory support for config (Issue #46)
description:
## Feature: XDG base directory support

### Source: Issue #46 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Support XDG Base Directory Specification for config location. Currently hardcoded to ~/.mempalace. Should respect XDG_CONFIG_HOME, XDG_DATA_HOME, XDG_STATE_HOME.

### Rust implementation
Use dirs crate which already supports XDG:
- Config: dirs::config_dir() / mempalace (XDG_CONFIG_HOME)
- Data: dirs::data_dir() / mempalace (XDG_DATA_HOME) for palace storage
- Fallback: ~/.mempalace for backward compatibility
- Migration path: detect old ~/.mempalace and offer to migrate

### Acceptance criteria
- Respects XDG_CONFIG_HOME on Linux
- Uses ~/Library/Application Support on macOS
- Uses AppData on Windows
- Backward compatible with ~/.mempalace
- Migration from old path works

### Test obligations
- Unit test: path resolution per platform
- Unit test: XDG env var override
- Unit test: fallback to ~/.mempalace

title: fix: Windows Unicode console crash in searcher (Issue #47)
description:
## Bug: UnicodeEncodeError on Windows cp1252 console

### Source: Issue #47 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
searcher.py uses Unicode box-drawing chars that crash on Windows cp1252 console. Need ASCII fallback or UTF-8 enforcement.

### Rust implementation
In Rust this is less of an issue since we control output encoding. But still:
- Use ASCII-safe characters for box drawing (-, |, +) or detect terminal capabilities
- Use owo-colors or colored crate which handles terminal detection
- Consider crossterm for cross-platform terminal handling
- Fallback to ASCII when terminal doesnt support Unicode

### Acceptance criteria
- Search output renders on Windows cmd.exe
- Search output renders on Windows PowerShell
- Search output renders on Unix terminals
- No panic on encoding errors

### Test obligations
- Unit test: output formatting with ASCII fallback

title: feat: import support for Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Aider sessions (Issue #59)
description:
## Feature: Additional AI tool session format importers

### Source: Issue #59 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Add normalize support for additional AI coding tool session formats beyond the current 5+. Candidate formats include:
- Cursor (SQLite state.vscdb)
- GitHub Copilot Chat (VS Code JSON)
- Windsurf/Codeium (local storage)
- Aider (.aider.chat.history.md markdown)
- Continue.dev (JSON sessions)

### Rust implementation
Each format gets a _try_<tool>() function in normalize.rs following the existing pattern. Auto-detection via unique field signatures or file paths.

### Acceptance criteria
- Each supported format auto-detected
- Conversation turns extracted correctly
- Standard transcript output format

### Test obligations
- Unit test per format with sample data
- Test auto-detection doesnt false-positive on other formats

title: feat: AI-driven init with smarter entity detection (Issue #26)
description:
## Feature: AI-driven init with smarter entity detection

### Source: Issue #26 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Current init picks up random strings as people/projects. Needs smarter detection that doesnt false-positive on common words. Init should focus on basic setup (DB location, wing structure) and let entity detection happen during mining with higher confidence thresholds.

### Rust implementation
- Raise confidence thresholds in entity_detector.rs
- Add negative patterns (common English words that look like names)
- Two-phase: basic init (config only) then entity learning during mining
- Better disambiguation UI when interactive

### Acceptance criteria
- Init doesnt flag common words as entities
- Entity detection during mining has higher accuracy
- Basic init works without entity detection
- Rejected entities stay rejected

### Test obligations
- Unit test: common words not detected as entities
- Unit test: real names detected correctly
- Integration test: init + mine workflow

title: feat: Codex CLI JSONL normalizer (PR #61)
description:
## Feature: OpenAI Codex CLI JSONL normalizer

### Source: PR #61 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
6th normalize format — parses OpenAI Codex CLI session files from ~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-*.jsonl. Each line is a JSON event with timestamp, type, and payload.

### Key event types
- session_meta — session ID, cwd, model, git info
- event_msg/user_message — real user prompts
- event_msg/agent_message — real assistant replies
- response_item — SKIPPED (contains synthetic context, duplicates)

### Design decisions
- Only event_msg entries extracted (not response_item)
- session_meta header required for format detection
- Avoids duplicate turns from response_item entries

### Rust implementation
Add _try_codex_jsonl() to normalize.rs. Parse JSONL line-by-line with serde_json. Filter by event type. Same pattern as other normalizers.

### Acceptance criteria
- Detects Codex JSONL format via session_meta presence
- Extracts user_message and agent_message turns
- Skips response_item entries
- Produces standard transcript format

### Test obligations
- Unit test: parse valid Codex JSONL
- Unit test: reject non-Codex JSONL
- Unit test: handle missing session_meta

title: feat: configurable exclude list for mining (Issue #56)
description:
## Feature: External exclude list for mining operations

### Source: Issue #56 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Allow --exclude flag or config YAML support for dir/file exclusion during mining. Currently SKIP_DIRS is hardcoded in source. Users need to customize what gets skipped.

### Rust implementation
- Add exclude_patterns field to config (Vec<String> of glob patterns)
- Add --exclude CLI flag (comma-separated or repeated)
- Use globset crate for pattern matching
- Merge config excludes with CLI excludes and built-in defaults

### Acceptance criteria
- --exclude flag works on CLI
- Config YAML exclude list works
- Glob patterns supported (*.log, build/**, etc.)
- Built-in defaults still apply unless overridden

### Test obligations
- Unit test: glob pattern matching
- Unit test: config + CLI merge
- Integration test: excluded dirs not mined

title: Port knowledge_graph.py → src/knowledge_graph.rs
description:
## Port: knowledge_graph.py (385 lines) → src/knowledge_graph.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/knowledge_graph.py

### What it does
Temporal entity-relationship graph stored in SQLite. Supports time-filtered queries, fact invalidation (valid_from → valid_to), confidence scores, and source tracking. Core structured knowledge layer alongside the vector palace.

### Key structures to port
- KnowledgeGraph struct with SQLite connection
- add_entity(name, type, properties) — insert entity
- add_triple(subject, predicate, object, confidence, source) — insert relationship
- invalidate(triple_id) — set valid_to timestamp
- query_entity(name) — get entity with relationships
- query_relationship(subject, predicate) — find matching triples
- timeline(entity) — chronological fact history
- stats() — graph statistics
- seed_from_entity_facts(facts) — bulk import

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- sqlite3 → rusqlite
- json → serde_json
- pathlib → std::path
- datetime → chrono

### Rust considerations
- rusqlite is the standard SQLite binding — well-maintained
- Use rusqlite::params! macro for parameterized queries
- Define Triple and Entity structs with serde derives
- Temporal queries use WHERE valid_to IS NULL for current facts
- Consider connection pooling (r2d2-sqlite) if concurrent access needed

### Acceptance criteria
- Creates SQLite schema on init
- Entities stored and retrieved correctly
- Triples with temporal validity work
- Invalidation sets valid_to correctly
- query_entity returns entity + active relationships
- timeline returns chronological history
- stats() reports accurate counts

### Test obligations
- Unit test: add/query entity
- Unit test: add/query triple
- Unit test: invalidation
- Unit test: temporal filtering
- Unit test: timeline ordering
- Unit test: stats accuracy
- All tests use in-memory SQLite (:memory:)

title: feat: agent-friendly zero-interactive setup (PR #33)
description:
## Feature: Agent-friendly zero-interactive setup

### Source: PR #33 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Makes MemPalace fully setup-able by AI agents with zero interactive prompts:
1. MEMPALACE_NONINTERACTIVE env var — skips all input() prompts
2. mempalace mine --auto — auto-detects project vs conversation files
3. Non-interactive entity confirmation fallback

### Rust implementation
- Add --auto flag to mine subcommand in cli.rs
- Check MEMPALACE_NONINTERACTIVE env var in onboarding.rs
- Auto-detect file type by scanning for known patterns (Claude/ChatGPT transcripts, Slack exports, etc.)
- Skip all interactive prompts when non-interactive mode active

### Acceptance criteria
- MEMPALACE_NONINTERACTIVE=1 skips all prompts
- --auto flag auto-detects mining mode
- Works correctly when stdin is /dev/null
- No behavior change in interactive mode

### Test obligations
- Unit test: auto-detection of file types
- Unit test: non-interactive mode skips prompts
- Integration test: mine --auto on mixed directory

title: Port cli.py → src/cli.rs
description:
## Port: cli.py (376 lines) → src/cli.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/cli.py

### What it does
Command-line interface with subcommands: init, mine, search, compress, wake-up, split, status. Routes to appropriate module functions. Uses argparse in Python.

### Key functions to port
- main() — argument parsing and dispatch
- cmd_init() — initialize palace
- cmd_mine() — mine projects and conversations
- cmd_search() — search palace
- cmd_wakeup() — generate wake-up context (L0+L1)
- cmd_split() — split mega transcript files
- cmd_status() — report palace status
- cmd_compress() — AAAK compression

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- argparse → clap (derive macro)
- Internal: config, miner, convo_miner, searcher, layers, split_mega_files, dialect

### Rust considerations
- clap derive macro for clean subcommand definitions
- Each subcommand as an enum variant with typed args
- Return anyhow::Result for error propagation
- Colored output via colored or owo-colors crate

### Acceptance criteria
- All 7 subcommands work
- Help text matches Python CLI
- Error messages are user-friendly
- Exit codes: 0 success, 1 error
- --help works for each subcommand

### Test obligations
- Integration test per subcommand
- Test --help output
- Test error cases (missing args, bad paths)
- Test with real palace directory

title: Project scaffold: Cargo.toml, src/lib.rs, module structure
description:
## Port: Project Infrastructure

Set up the Rust project skeleton that all other modules will build on.

### What to create
- Cargo.toml with workspace dependencies:
  - chromadb (or equivalent vector DB client — investigate qdrant-client or chromadb Rust bindings)
  - rusqlite for SQLite (knowledge_graph)
  - serde + serde_json for JSON handling
  - clap for CLI
  - tokio for async runtime
  - regex for pattern matching
  - thiserror for error types
  - tracing for logging
- src/lib.rs — re-export all modules
- src/main.rs — delegate to cli module
- Module declarations matching Python structure

### Rust module layout
src/lib.rs, main.rs, config.rs, normalize.rs, miner.rs, convo_miner.rs, searcher.rs, layers.rs, dialect.rs, knowledge_graph.rs, palace_graph.rs, mcp_server.rs, cli.rs, onboarding.rs, entity_registry.rs, entity_detector.rs, general_extractor.rs, room_detector_local.rs, spellcheck.rs, split_mega_files.rs

### Why
Every other bead depends on this. The module structure must be in place before any file can be ported. Choosing the right crate equivalents for Python dependencies (chromadb, yaml, autocorrect) is a critical early decision.

### Acceptance criteria
- cargo check passes with empty module stubs
- All modules declared in lib.rs
- Dependencies compile
- Error type defined in a shared location

### Test obligations
- cargo check succeeds
- cargo test runs (even if no tests yet)
close_reason: Completed: Project scaffold with crates/ workspace structure, Cargo.toml with usearch, core and cli crates, all module stubs compile successfully.

title: feat: Hermes memory provider integration (PR #3)
description:
## Feature: Hermes memory provider integration

### Source: PR #3 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Native MemPalace memory provider for Hermes (open-source AI agent framework). Drop-in plugin implementing Hermes MemoryProvider ABC. One-command installer. Auto-files every conversation turn into palace.

### Rust implementation
Create integrations/hermes/ module or separate crate. Implement Hermes MemoryProvider trait (if Rust Hermes SDK exists) or provide HTTP adapter. Include install command in CLI.

### Acceptance criteria
- Hermes plugin loads and connects to palace
- Conversation turns auto-filed
- Install command works
- Backfill existing sessions

### Test obligations
- Unit test: memory provider interface
- Integration test: file and retrieve a turn

title: fix: security hardening across modules (PR #34)
description:
## Fix: Security hardening

### Source: PR #34 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Security hardening across 6 files from full codebase audit: shell injection prevention, SSL bypass removal, error leak prevention, read-only MCP mode.

### Rust implementation considerations
Many Python-specific issues dont apply to Rust (no shell injection via subprocess, no SSL bypass). But the principles carry over:
- No shell command execution with user input (use Command::new, not shell strings)
- No sensitive data in error messages
- Read-only MCP mode: separate read/write tool sets
- Input validation on all MCP tool parameters
- MD5 usedforsecurity=false equivalent: document non-crypto usage in comments

### Acceptance criteria
- No shell injection vectors
- Error messages dont leak internal paths or data
- MCP read-only mode available
- All user inputs validated

### Test obligations
- Unit test: input validation on MCP tools
- Unit test: error messages dont contain sensitive data
- Test read-only MCP mode

title: Port spellcheck.py → src/spellcheck.rs
description:
## Port: spellcheck.py (270 lines) → src/spellcheck.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/spellcheck.py

### What it does
Name-aware spellcheck — corrects user text while preserving proper nouns from entity registry, technical terms, CamelCase, URLs. Uses system word list and optional autocorrect library.

### Key functions to port
- spellcheck_user_text(text, registry) → corrected text
- spellcheck_transcript_line(line, registry) → corrected line
- spellcheck_transcript(text, registry) → corrected full transcript
- _should_skip(word) → bool — skip URLs, CamelCase, known entities
- _load_known_names(registry) → HashSet<String>
- _edit_distance(a, b) → usize — Levenshtein distance

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- re → regex
- autocorrect (optional) → consider symspell or custom implementation
- /usr/share/dict/words → embed word list or use system file

### Rust considerations
- Edit distance: use strsim crate (already needed for palace_graph fuzzy matching)
- Word list: embed via include_str! or load from system
- Skip logic is straightforward pattern matching
- Optional dependency: feature-gate the spellcheck crate

### Acceptance criteria
- Common misspellings corrected
- Entity names preserved (not "corrected")
- CamelCase preserved
- URLs preserved
- Technical terms preserved

### Test obligations
- Unit test: basic correction
- Unit test: entity name preservation
- Unit test: CamelCase preservation
- Unit test: URL preservation
- Unit test: edit distance calculation

title: Port searcher.py → src/searcher.rs
description:
## Port: searcher.py (143 lines) → src/searcher.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/searcher.py

### What it does
Semantic search against the palace via ChromaDB vectors. Supports wing/room filtering, returns verbatim text with similarity scores. Two entry points: search() for CLI and search_memories() for programmatic use.

### Key functions to port
- search(query, wing, room, n_results) — CLI search entry
- search_memories(query, wing, room, n_results, config) — programmatic search

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- chromadb → same vector DB client
- pathlib → std::path

### Rust considerations
- Smallest module — good candidate for early implementation
- Search results should be a well-defined struct (SearchResult with text, score, metadata)
- Wing/room filtering via ChromaDB where clause

### Acceptance criteria
- Queries return ranked results with scores
- Wing filter works
- Room filter works
- Combined wing+room filter works
- Empty results handled gracefully

### Test obligations
- Integration test: store data, search, verify results
- Unit test: result formatting
- Test wing/room filtering
close_reason: Ported searcher.py to searcher.rs with full wing/room filtering, naive full-text search fallback (vector search pending embeddings), SearchResult/SearchResponse types, CLI search formatting, duplicate detection, and comprehensive tests.

title: feat: SoulForge session normalizer (PR #52)
description:
## Feature: SoulForge JSONL session normalizer

### Source: PR #52 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
7th normalize format — parses SoulForge JSONL sessions. Auto-detects via unique fields (segments, toolCalls, durationMs). Extracts readable text from both flat and segments-based messages. Summarizes tool calls as one-liners. Skips system messages unless shown in chat.

### Rust implementation
Add _try_soulforge_jsonl() to normalize.rs. Detect via unique field presence. Extract from segments array or flat message field.

### Acceptance criteria
- Auto-detects SoulForge format
- Extracts from segments-based and flat messages
- Summarizes tool calls
- Skips system messages

### Test obligations
- Unit test: full session extraction
- Unit test: malformed input
- Unit test: missing tool refs
- Unit test: empty segments fallback
- Unit test: truncation handling

title: fix: status command 10K drawer limit + unexpanded palace_path (Issue #40)
description:
## Bug: status command capped at 10K drawers

### Source: Issue #40 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
miner.py status() uses hardcoded limit=10000 in ChromaDB get(). Palaces with >10K drawers show incorrect totals. Also palace_path not expanded (shows ~ instead of full path).

### Rust implementation
- Use collection.count() first, then get with actual count
- Expand ~ in palace_path display (shellexpand crate or manual)
- Consider pagination for very large palaces

### Acceptance criteria
- Status shows correct count for palaces >10K drawers
- Palace path fully expanded in output
- No performance regression for large palaces

### Test obligations
- Unit test: status with >10K mock entries
- Unit test: path expansion

title: Port config.py → src/config.rs
description:
## Port: config.py (150 lines) → src/config.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/config.py

### What it does
MempalaceConfig class — loads configuration from env vars > config file (~/.mempalace/config.json) > defaults. Provides palace_path, collection_name, people_map, topic_wings, hall_keywords. Has init() to create dirs and save_people_map().

### Key structures to port
- MempalaceConfig struct with fields: palace_path (PathBuf), collection_name (String), people_map (HashMap), topic_wings (HashMap), hall_keywords (Vec<String>)
- Config loading priority: env vars → config file → defaults
- init() creates ~/.mempalace/ directory structure
- save_people_map() persists people map to config

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- json → serde_json
- os/pathlib → std::path, dirs crate for home dir
- No external services

### Rust considerations
- Use serde Deserialize/Serialize for config struct
- Use dirs::home_dir() for ~/.mempalace path
- Consider config crate or manual JSON loading
- All methods should return Result<T, Error>

### Acceptance criteria
- MempalaceConfig loads from JSON file
- Env var overrides work
- Default values match Python defaults
- init() creates directory structure
- Round-trip: load → save → load produces same config

### Test obligations
- Unit test: default config values
- Unit test: config from file (tempdir)
- Unit test: env var override
- Unit test: init creates dirs
- Port test_config.py (33 lines) tests
close_reason: Completed: Full config.rs port with load/save/init/people_map, matches Python behavior

title: feat: non-Latin script support / Unicode-aware processing (PR #28)
description:
## Feature: Non-Latin script support (Unicode-aware)

### Source: PR #28 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Replace ASCII-only patterns ([A-Z][a-z]) with Unicode-aware equivalents for universal script support. Modular per-language architecture starting with Russian/Cyrillic. Language module system extracts language-specific data (verb patterns, stopwords, emotion/flag signals) from entity_detector and dialect into languages/ package.

### Rust implementation
- Use regex crate with Unicode support (enabled by default)
- Replace ASCII character classes with Unicode: \p{Lu}\p{Ll} for upper/lowercase
- Create languages/ module with trait-based language support
- Implement Russian language module as first non-Latin language
- 33 person verb patterns for Russian

### Affected modules
- entity_detector.rs — Unicode regex for name detection
- dialect.rs — Unicode-aware AAAK compression
- New: languages/ module with Language trait

### Acceptance criteria
- Entity detection works with Cyrillic names
- AAAK compression handles non-Latin text
- Language modules are pluggable
- Existing Latin behavior unchanged

### Test obligations
- Unit test: Cyrillic entity detection
- Unit test: AAAK with Russian text
- Unit test: language module loading

title: perf: fix slow mining on Windows (Issue #19)
description:
## Bug: Super slow mining on Windows

### Source: Issue #19 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Mining is extremely slow on Windows — 45 min for 60/3276 files on a NextJS project. Root causes likely: per-file ChromaDB calls, no batching, file lock contention, antivirus interference.

### Rust implementation
This is largely addressed by the batch I/O bead (mr-tks) but additional Windows-specific considerations:
- Use async file I/O (tokio::fs) to avoid blocking on Windows file locks
- Batch ChromaDB inserts (already in mr-tks)
- Pre-filter SKIP_DIRS before walking (dont even stat skipped dirs)
- Consider rayon for parallel file processing
- Progress bar with indicatif crate for user feedback during long mines

### Acceptance criteria
- Mining 3000+ files completes in reasonable time (<5 min)
- Progress indicator shows during mining
- Windows file locks handled gracefully

### Test obligations
- Benchmark: mine large directory, measure time
- Test progress reporting

title: CI/CD + release workflow + install.sh (/release)
description:
## CI/CD Pipeline + Release Workflow + curl-pipe Installer

### Source: /release skill (rust-cli-scaffold)

### What it does
Add GitHub Actions CI, cross-platform release workflow, and production-grade install.sh to the Rust project. Three files:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml — fmt + clippy + test on 3 OS matrix
- .github/workflows/release.yml — cross-compile 5 targets on vX.Y.Z tag
- install.sh — curl-pipe installer with checksum verification

### CI Workflow (ci.yml)
- Trigger: push to main/dev, PRs to main
- Matrix: ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest
- Steps: checkout, rust-toolchain@stable (with rustfmt, clippy), rust-cache, cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy -D warnings, cargo test --all-features

### Release Workflow (release.yml)
- Trigger: vX.Y.Z tag push
- Build matrix (5 targets):
  - x86_64-unknown-linux-musl (cross)
  - aarch64-unknown-linux-musl (cross)
  - x86_64-apple-darwin (native)
  - aarch64-apple-darwin (native)
  - x86_64-pc-windows-msvc (native)
- Artifacts: .tar.gz (Unix) or .zip (Windows) + .sha256 sidecar
- Release: softprops/action-gh-release@v2 with generate_release_notes

### install.sh Features
- Safety: set -euo pipefail, umask 022
- Platform detection: Linux/macOS/Windows, x86_64/aarch64
- Version resolution: GitHub API with redirect fallback
- Download: retry + resume + proxy support
- Checksum verification: SHA-256 sidecar
- Atomic binary installation
- Concurrent install locking
- --easy-mode PATH update
- --from-source fallback (cargo build)
- --uninstall flag
- --verify self-test
- curl|bash safety wrapper (buffer entire script)

### Acceptance criteria
- CI passes on all 3 OS
- Release builds all 5 targets
- install.sh works on Linux, macOS, Windows (Git Bash)
- Checksum verification passes
- --from-source builds correctly
- --uninstall removes cleanly
- README updated with install command

### Test obligations
- CI workflow runs successfully
- Release workflow produces all 5 artifacts
- install.sh tested on Linux (at minimum)
- Checksum verification tested
- Uninstall tested

### Final step
After all tests pass:
git tag v0.1.0 && git push origin main --tags

title: feat: multilingual search with configurable embedding model (Issue #50)
description:
## Feature: Multilingual search support

### Source: Issue #50 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Default embedding model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) is English-centric. Cross-lingual search quality degrades. Need configurable embedding model support, ideally with multilingual default option.

### Rust implementation
- Make embedding model configurable in config.rs
- Support paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2 or similar
- Config field: embedding_model in mempalace config
- Pass model name to vector DB client

### Acceptance criteria
- Embedding model configurable via config
- Multilingual model option available
- Cross-lingual search quality improved with multilingual model
- Default model unchanged for backward compatibility

### Test obligations
- Integration test: search with multilingual model
- Test cross-lingual query (English query, non-English content)

title: Port onboarding.py → src/onboarding.rs
description:
## Port: onboarding.py (490 lines) → src/onboarding.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/onboarding.py

### What it does
Guided first-run setup — asks about people/projects, generates AAAK bootstrap and wing config. Interactive terminal flow with auto-detection of entities from existing files.

### Key functions to port
- _ask_mode() — choose setup mode (guided/auto/skip)
- _ask_people() — interactive people entry
- _ask_projects() — interactive project entry
- _ask_wings() — configure wing structure
- _auto_detect() — scan files for entities
- _warn_ambiguous() — flag ambiguous names
- _generate_aaak_bootstrap() — create initial AAAK entity codes
- run_onboarding() — main interactive flow
- quick_setup() — non-interactive defaults

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- pathlib → std::path
- Internal: entity_registry, entity_detector

### Rust considerations
- Interactive prompts: use dialoguer crate (Select, Input, Confirm)
- Auto-detection delegates to entity_detector
- AAAK bootstrap generates entity_registry.json
- Consider separating interactive (dialoguer) from logic (pure functions)

### Acceptance criteria
- Guided mode walks through all steps
- Auto mode detects entities and creates config
- Quick setup creates sensible defaults
- AAAK bootstrap generates valid entity registry
- Wing config saved as valid YAML

### Test obligations
- Unit test: _generate_aaak_bootstrap with known entities
- Unit test: quick_setup produces valid config
- Unit test: _auto_detect with temp directory
- Integration test: full onboarding flow (mock stdin)

title: Port split_mega_files.py → src/split_mega_files.rs
description:
## Port: split_mega_files.py (295 lines) → src/split_mega_files.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/split_mega_files.py

### What it does
Splits concatenated transcript mega-files into per-session files. Distinguishes true session starts from context restores. Extracts timestamps, people, and subjects from session boundaries.

### Key functions to port
- is_true_session_start(line, context) → bool — distinguish real starts from restores
- find_session_boundaries(text) → Vec<Boundary> — locate split points
- extract_timestamp(header) → Option<DateTime> — parse session timestamp
- extract_people(header) → Vec<String> — find participant names
- extract_subject(header) → Option<String> — extract session topic
- split_file(path, output_dir) → Vec<PathBuf> — main split operation
- main() — CLI entry point (standalone usage)

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- argparse → clap (if standalone) or just function args
- json → serde_json
- re → regex
- pathlib → std::path

### Rust considerations
- Regex for session boundary detection
- File I/O with BufReader/BufWriter for large files
- Output filenames from timestamp + subject
- Consider streaming approach for very large files

### Acceptance criteria
- Correctly identifies session boundaries
- Distinguishes true starts from context restores
- Extracts metadata (timestamp, people, subject)
- Output files are valid standalone transcripts
- Handles edge cases (single session, empty file)

### Test obligations
- Unit test: is_true_session_start
- Unit test: find_session_boundaries
- Unit test: extract_timestamp/people/subject
- Integration test: split a known mega-file, verify output count and content

title: Port miner.py → src/miner.rs
description:
## Port: miner.py (418 lines) → src/miner.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/miner.py

### What it does
Mines project files (code, docs, notes) into the palace. Scans directories, chunks text by paragraph with overlap, stores to ChromaDB with metadata (wing, room, source_file, chunk_index, filed_at).

### Key functions to port
- load_config() — reads mempalace.yaml for wing/room mappings
- detect_room(path) — maps file path to room name
- chunk_text(text, size=800, overlap=100) — splits text into overlapping chunks
- get_collection() — gets/creates ChromaDB collection
- file_already_mined(collection, path) — dedup check via metadata query
- add_drawer(collection, text, metadata) — stores chunk with MD5 ID
- process_file(path, wing, collection) — reads file, chunks, stores
- scan_project(path, wing) — walks directory tree
- mine() — main entry point
- status() — reports palace statistics

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- chromadb → chromadb/qdrant Rust client (critical dependency decision)
- yaml → serde_yaml
- pathlib → std::path, walkdir crate
- datetime → chrono
- collections → std::collections
- MD5 hashing for drawer IDs → md5 crate

### Rust considerations
- ChromaDB client choice is critical — may need HTTP client to ChromaDB server
- walkdir crate for recursive directory scanning
- Chunking logic is straightforward string slicing
- Consider async for file I/O

### Acceptance criteria
- Scans directory tree and finds files
- Chunks text with correct overlap
- Stores to vector DB with correct metadata
- Deduplication works (same file not re-mined)
- Status reports accurate counts

### Test obligations
- Unit test: chunk_text with various sizes
- Unit test: detect_room mapping
- Integration test: mine a temp directory, verify stored
- Port test_miner.py (37 lines) tests

title: Port dialect.py → src/dialect.rs
description:
## Port: dialect.py (1051 lines) → src/dialect.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/dialect.py

### What it does
AAAK (compressed symbolic memory) encoding/decoding. Achieves ~30x lossless compression via entity codes, emotion markers, and structured flags (ORIGIN, CORE, PIVOT, DECISION, TECHNICAL). Largest module in the codebase.

### Key structures to port
- Dialect struct with entity registry reference
- compress(text) → compressed AAAK string
- encode_zettel(text) → zettel-format encoding
- encode_file(path) → encode entire file
- compress_file(path) → compress entire file
- compress_all(dir) → batch compress
- generate_layer1() → generate L1 summary from compressed data
- decode(aaak_text) → decompress back to readable text
- compression_stats() → ratio and size metrics

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- json → serde_json
- re → regex crate
- pathlib → std::path
- entity_registry module (internal)

### Rust considerations
- Heavy regex usage — use regex crate with compiled patterns (lazy_static or once_cell)
- String manipulation intensive — consider Cow<str> for zero-copy where possible
- Entity code substitution is essentially a find-replace pipeline
- Emotion markers are pattern-based classification
- This is the largest file — consider splitting into submodules (compress.rs, decode.rs, flags.rs)

### Acceptance criteria
- compress() produces valid AAAK output
- decode() round-trips: decode(compress(text)) ≈ text
- Entity codes substituted correctly
- Emotion markers detected and encoded
- Flags (ORIGIN, CORE, etc.) applied correctly
- compression_stats() reports accurate ratios
- ~30x compression ratio maintained

### Test obligations
- Unit test: compress/decode round-trip
- Unit test: entity code substitution
- Unit test: emotion marker detection
- Unit test: flag application
- Unit test: compression ratio calculation
- Test with real conversation samples

title: Port palace_graph.py → src/palace_graph.rs
description:
## Port: palace_graph.py (228 lines) → src/palace_graph.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/palace_graph.py

### What it does
Graph traversal layer — rooms as nodes, shared content as edges (tunnels). BFS traversal between rooms, tunnel detection across wings, fuzzy room name matching.

### Key functions to port
- build_graph(collection) → HashMap<String, Vec<String>> adjacency list
- traverse(graph, start, end) → Vec<String> BFS path
- find_tunnels(collection) → rooms that span multiple wings
- graph_stats(graph) → node/edge counts, connected components
- _fuzzy_match(a, b) → approximate room name matching

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- chromadb → vector DB client
- collections → std::collections (VecDeque for BFS)
- config module (internal)

### Rust considerations
- BFS is straightforward with VecDeque
- Adjacency list as HashMap<String, HashSet<String>>
- Fuzzy matching: use strsim crate (Levenshtein, Jaro-Winkler)
- Graph building queries ChromaDB for room metadata

### Acceptance criteria
- build_graph produces correct adjacency list
- BFS traversal finds shortest path
- Tunnels detected (rooms in multiple wings)
- graph_stats reports accurate metrics
- Fuzzy matching handles typos/variants

### Test obligations
- Unit test: BFS traversal (known graph)
- Unit test: tunnel detection
- Unit test: graph stats
- Unit test: fuzzy matching thresholds
- Integration test: build graph from populated palace

title: feat: OpenCode SQLite session support (PR #23)
description:
## Feature: OpenCode SQLite session database support

### Source: PR #23 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
8th normalize format — reads OpenCode sessions from SQLite database. Uses directory column to create temp-dir structure matching other tools. Extracts conversation turns from session table.

### Rust implementation
Add OpenCode SQLite reader to normalize.rs (or separate module). Use rusqlite to query session table. Create temp directory structure from dir column. Convert to standard transcript format.

### Acceptance criteria
- Reads OpenCode SQLite database
- Extracts sessions with directory context
- Produces standard transcript format
- Handles missing/corrupt databases gracefully

### Test obligations
- Unit test: parse valid OpenCode DB (in-memory SQLite)
- Unit test: handle missing tables
- Unit test: handle empty sessions

title: feat: doctor command — palace health check (PR #36)
description:
## Feature: mempalace doctor — palace health check

### Source: PR #36 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
New CLI subcommand with 6 independent checks: ChromaDB connectivity, orphan drawers, duplicate drawers, knowledge graph dangling references, identity.txt presence, config.json validity. Colorized output, --no-color flag, non-zero exit on failure for CI.

### Rust implementation
Add doctor.rs module and doctor subcommand to cli.rs. Each check is an independent function returning a CheckResult. Colorized output via colored/owo-colors crate.

### Checks to implement
1. ChromaDB/vector DB connectivity
2. Orphan drawers (drawers with no matching source file)
3. Duplicate drawers (same content hash)
4. Knowledge graph dangling references
5. identity.txt presence
6. config.json validity

### Acceptance criteria
- All 6 checks run independently
- Colorized output (green/red/yellow)
- --no-color flag works
- Non-zero exit code on failure
- Read-only — no writes

### Test obligations
- Unit test per check
- Integration test: full doctor run on healthy palace
- Integration test: doctor detects known issues

title: fix: AAAK token estimate incorrect (Issue #43)
description:
## Bug: Incorrect token estimate for AAAK

### Source: Issue #43 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
The AAAK compression stats report token counts that dont match actual tokenizer output (e.g. OpenAI tokenizer). The chars/4 approximation is too rough for compressed AAAK text which has unusual token boundaries.

### Rust implementation
- Use tiktoken-rs crate for accurate token counting (cl100k_base encoding)
- Or provide multiple estimates (chars/4 approx + tiktoken exact)
- Feature-gate tiktoken dependency if its heavy

### Acceptance criteria
- Token estimates within 10% of actual tokenizer output
- compression_stats() reports accurate token counts
- Both pre and post compression token counts correct

### Test obligations
- Unit test: token count vs known tokenizer output
- Unit test: compression ratio with accurate tokens

title: Port Python tests → Rust tests
description:
## Port: Python test suite → Rust tests

### Python reference
Files:
- tests/test_normalize.py (32 lines) — plain text, Claude JSON, empty files
- tests/test_config.py (33 lines) — default config, config from file, env override, init
- tests/test_convo_miner.py (27 lines) — conversation mining, search verification
- tests/test_miner.py (37 lines) — project mining with ChromaDB verification

### What to create
Rust test modules — both unit tests (inline #[cfg(test)] mod tests) and integration tests (tests/ directory).

### Test structure
```
tests/
  test_normalize.rs — port test_normalize.py
  test_config.rs — port test_config.py
  test_convo_miner.rs — port test_convo_miner.py
  test_miner.rs — port test_miner.py
  common/mod.rs — shared test fixtures (temp dirs, sample data)
```

### Rust considerations
- Use tempdir crate for temporary test directories
- Use assert_eq!, assert! macros
- ChromaDB tests need test instance or mock
- Config tests use temp config files
- Consider test fixtures as const strings

### Acceptance criteria
- All Python test cases ported
- Tests pass with cargo test
- Integration tests use real (temp) storage
- No test pollution between runs (clean temp dirs)

### Test obligations
- This IS the test bead — all tests must pass
- Coverage for: normalize, config, miner, convo_miner
- Each module also has inline unit tests (defined in per-module beads)

title: Port entity_registry.py → src/entity_registry.rs
description:
## Port: entity_registry.py (644 lines) → src/entity_registry.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/entity_registry.py

### What it does
Persistent personal entity registry — maps names to AAAK codes, handles ambiguous names, optional Wikipedia lookup for unknown words. Stores people, projects, and ambiguous flags. JSON persistence at ~/.mempalace/entity_registry.json.

### Key structures to port
- EntityRegistry struct with entries HashMap
- Entry struct: name, code, entity_type, aliases, ambiguous flag, confidence
- load() / save() — JSON persistence
- seed(entries) — bulk import
- lookup(name) → Option<Entry> — find by name or alias
- research(name) — Wikipedia lookup for unknown entities
- confirm_research(name, is_entity) — user confirms detection
- learn_from_text(text) — extract entities from text
- extract_people_from_query(query) — find known people in search query
- extract_unknown_candidates(text) — find potential new entities
- summary() — registry statistics

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- json → serde_json
- re → regex
- urllib → reqwest (for Wikipedia API)
- pathlib → std::path

### Rust considerations
- Serde for JSON serialization of registry
- reqwest for optional Wikipedia HTTP calls (feature-gated)
- Case-insensitive lookup via .to_lowercase()
- Alias matching needs fuzzy/partial support

### Acceptance criteria
- Load/save round-trip preserves all data
- Lookup by name and alias works
- Seed imports correctly
- Wikipedia research optional (graceful offline)
- extract_people_from_query finds known entities in text
- extract_unknown_candidates identifies capitalized words not in registry

### Test obligations
- Unit test: load/save round-trip
- Unit test: lookup by name and alias
- Unit test: seed import
- Unit test: extract_people_from_query
- Unit test: extract_unknown_candidates
- Test offline behavior (no Wikipedia)

title: Port __init__.py + __main__.py → src/lib.rs + src/main.rs
description:
## Port: __init__.py (7 lines) + __main__.py (5 lines) → src/lib.rs + src/main.rs

### Python reference
Files: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/__init__.py, __main__.py

### What it does
__init__.py: Package init, exports main and __version__.
__main__.py: Allows python -m mempalace, calls cli.main().

### Rust equivalent
- src/lib.rs: pub mod declarations for all modules, re-exports
- src/main.rs: fn main() calls cli::main()
- Version from Cargo.toml (env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"))

### Why separate bead
This is the glue that ties all modules together. Must be updated as each module is ported. Depends on the scaffold bead but is distinct — scaffold creates stubs, this wires them up with real exports.

### Acceptance criteria
- lib.rs exports all public types
- main.rs delegates to cli
- cargo run --help works
- Version string accessible

### Test obligations
- cargo build succeeds
- cargo run -- --help shows usage

title: Port mcp_server.py → src/mcp_server.rs
description:
## Port: mcp_server.py (769 lines) → src/mcp_server.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/mcp_server.py

### What it does
MCP (Model Context Protocol) server exposing 24 tools for Claude Code integration. Read tools: status, list_wings, list_rooms, get_taxonomy, search, check_duplicate, traverse, find_tunnels, graph_stats, kg_query, kg_timeline, kg_stats, diary_read. Write tools: add_drawer, delete_drawer, kg_add, kg_invalidate, diary_write. Also includes AAAK dialect spec and agent diary functionality.

### Key structures to port
- MCP server setup with tool registration
- 24 tool handler functions
- Tool input/output schemas
- AAAK auto-teach prompt
- Agent diary (read/write to file)
- Error handling per tool

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- chromadb → vector DB client
- json → serde_json
- logging → tracing
- datetime → chrono
- Internal: searcher, palace_graph, knowledge_graph, dialect

### Rust considerations
- Use rmcp or mcp-rs crate for MCP protocol (investigate availability)
- If no MCP crate exists, implement JSON-RPC over stdio manually
- Each tool is a handler function with typed input/output
- Consider tower-based service architecture
- This is the integration point — depends on almost every other module

### Acceptance criteria
- MCP server starts and responds to initialize
- All 24 tools registered and callable
- Search tool returns correct results
- KG tools read/write correctly
- Diary read/write works
- AAAK spec included in tool descriptions
- Error responses are well-formed MCP errors

### Test obligations
- Unit test per tool handler (mock dependencies)
- Integration test: full MCP request/response cycle
- Test error handling for each tool
- Test with Claude Code MCP client if possible

title: feat: palace_db singleton + consolidate MCP tools 19→14 (PR #25)
description:
## Feature: Centralize ChromaDB into palace_db singleton, consolidate MCP tools

### Source: PR #25 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Major architectural improvement:
1. Extract shared palace_db.rs module — singleton ChromaDB/vector DB access
2. Consolidate MCP tools from 19 to 14 (merge overlapping tools)
3. Add constants.rs for shared constants (chunk sizes, search defaults, etc.)
4. Remove dead code and duplications

### Rust implementation
- New palace_db.rs: PalaceDb struct wrapping vector DB client, shared across all modules
- Connection pooling or Arc<Mutex<>> for thread safety
- Constants in a dedicated module (or config.rs)
- MCP tool consolidation in mcp_server.rs

### Why this matters for Rust port
This is the RIGHT architecture to port to — don't port the pre-PR#25 scattered ChromaDB access. Build the singleton pattern from the start.

### Acceptance criteria
- Single PalaceDb struct used by all modules
- No direct ChromaDB client creation outside palace_db
- Constants centralized
- MCP tools consolidated (14 not 19)

### Test obligations
- Unit test: PalaceDb creation and access
- Unit test: thread-safe concurrent access
- Integration test: multiple modules sharing PalaceDb

title: refactor: centralize storage access (PR #17) + constants (PR #15)
description:
## Refactor: Centralize storage and constants

### Source: PR #17 + PR #15 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
PR #17: Shared storage module for collection access, filters, and semantic queries. Makes miner, searcher, and layers use configured collection name consistently.
PR #15: Extract magic numbers into constants — chunk sizes, snippet limits, search defaults, graph traversal caps.

### Rust implementation
This overlaps heavily with the palace_db singleton bead (mr-9z5). In Rust, implement as:
- constants.rs or include in config.rs: pub const CHUNK_SIZE: usize = 800; pub const CHUNK_OVERLAP: usize = 100; etc.
- Storage access centralized in palace_db.rs

### Acceptance criteria
- No magic numbers in module code
- All storage access through palace_db
- Collection name from config, not hardcoded

### Test obligations

- Integration test: storage module used consistently

title: Port normalize.py → src/normalize.rs
description:
## Port: normalize.py (254 lines) → src/normalize.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/normalize.py

### What it does
Converts 5 chat export formats to a standard transcript format. Formats: Claude Code JSONL, Claude.ai JSON, ChatGPT JSON, Slack JSON, plain text. Pure text processing — no external dependencies.

### Key functions to port
- normalize(path) → String — main entry, tries each format
- _try_normalize_json() — attempts JSON-based formats
- _try_claude_code_jsonl() — Claude Code JSONL format
- _try_claude_ai_json() — Claude.ai export format
- _try_chatgpt_json() — ChatGPT export format
- _try_slack_json() — Slack export format
- _extract_content() — extracts text from message content blocks
- _messages_to_transcript() — converts message list to transcript string

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- json → serde_json
- pathlib → std::path
- No external services — pure transformation

### Rust considerations
- Use enum for format detection with try-parse pattern
- serde_json::Value for flexible JSON parsing
- Return Result<String, NormalizeError> with per-format error variants
- Consider using serde with untagged enum for auto-detection

### Acceptance criteria
- All 5 formats normalize correctly
- Unknown formats return error (not panic)
- Empty files handled gracefully
- Output matches Python output for same input

### Test obligations
- Unit test per format (Claude Code, Claude.ai, ChatGPT, Slack, plain text)
- Unit test: empty file
- Unit test: malformed JSON
- Port test_normalize.py (32 lines) tests

title: feat: episodic memory — track retrieval outcomes (Issue #10)
description:
## Feature: Episodic memory layer

### Source: Issue #10 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Track retrieval outcomes so the palace learns whats useful. When a memory is retrieved and user confirms/denies its relevance, record that signal. Over time, boost frequently-helpful memories and demote misleading ones.

### Rust implementation
- New episode table in SQLite (knowledge_graph DB or separate)
- Fields: drawer_id, query, outcome (helpful/unhelpful/neutral), timestamp
- Retrieval scoring: blend semantic similarity with historical helpfulness
- MCP tools: mempalace_feedback(drawer_id, outcome)

### Acceptance criteria
- Feedback recorded per retrieval
- Retrieval scoring incorporates feedback history
- Helpful memories ranked higher over time
- MCP tool for recording feedback

### Test obligations
- Unit test: record and query feedback
- Unit test: scoring with feedback history
- Integration test: feedback improves ranking

title: feat: knowledge graph auto-resolve conflicting triples (Issue #11)
description:
## Feature: Auto-resolve conflicting triples in knowledge graph

### Source: Issue #11 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
When a new triple conflicts with an existing one (e.g. Alice works_at Acme vs Alice works_at NewCo), automatically invalidate the old triple by setting valid_to. Currently conflicts are only detected, not resolved.

### Rust implementation
In knowledge_graph.rs add_triple():
- Before inserting, query for existing active triples with same subject+predicate
- If found and object differs, set valid_to on old triple
- Insert new triple with valid_from = now
- Log the resolution for audit

### Acceptance criteria
- Conflicting triples auto-resolved (old invalidated)
- Resolution logged
- Non-conflicting triples unaffected
- Timeline shows both old and new facts

### Test obligations
- Unit test: add conflicting triple, verify old invalidated
- Unit test: non-conflicting triple preserved
- Unit test: timeline shows history

title: feat: mine-device command for machine-wide discovery (PR #51)
description:
## Feature: mine-device command

### Source: PR #51 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
New CLI subcommand that scans the entire machine for AI conversation sessions across all supported tools (Claude Code, ChatGPT, Codex, SoulForge, OpenCode, etc.). Auto-discovers session directories and mines them all.

### Rust implementation
Add mine_device subcommand to cli.rs. Scan known paths:
- ~/.claude/ (Claude Code)
- ~/.codex/sessions/ (Codex)
- ~/Library/Application Support/ or ~/.config/ (various tools)
- Platform-specific paths

### Acceptance criteria
- Discovers sessions from all supported tools
- Mines discovered sessions into palace
- Reports what was found and mined
- Handles missing/inaccessible paths gracefully

### Test obligations

- Integration test: mine from temp directory structure

title: Port convo_miner.py → src/convo_miner.rs
description:
## Port: convo_miner.py (401 lines) → src/convo_miner.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/convo_miner.py

### What it does
Mines conversation exports into the palace. Chunks by exchange pair (Q+A) or by paragraph. Detects rooms from conversation content. Two extract modes: exchange (Q+A pairs) and general (5 memory types via general_extractor).

### Key functions to port
- chunk_exchanges(text) — splits transcript into Q+A exchange pairs
- _chunk_by_exchange(text) — exchange-pair chunking
- _chunk_by_paragraph(text) — paragraph-based chunking

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)

- normalize module (internal)

- datetime → chrono
- collections → std::collections

### Rust considerations
- Shares ChromaDB client with miner.rs — extract common DB trait/module

### Acceptance criteria
- Normalizes conversation files before mining
- Exchange-pair chunking produces correct Q+A pairs
- Paragraph chunking works as fallback

- Deduplication prevents re-mining

- Unit test: paragraph chunking

- Integration test: mine conversations, verify search
- Port test_convo_miner.py (27 lines) tests

title: feat: AAAK as inter-agent compression language (Issue #4)
description:
## Feature: AAAK as agent-to-agent compression language

### Source: Issue #4 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Use AAAK compression for token-efficient communication between LLMs. Compress prompts before sending to API, decompress responses. Could save significant tokens on long conversations.

### Rust implementation
- Expose compress/decompress as library functions (already in dialect.rs)
- Add MCP tools: mempalace_compress(text) and mempalace_decompress(text)
- Consider middleware/proxy mode that auto-compresses context

### Acceptance criteria
- compress() and decompress() available as MCP tools
- Round-trip preserves meaning
- Token savings measurable

### Test obligations
- Unit test: compress/decompress round-trip
- Benchmark: token savings on sample conversations

title: Port room_detector_local.py → src/room_detector_local.rs
description:
## Port: room_detector_local.py (301 lines) → src/room_detector_local.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/room_detector_local.py

### What it does
Maps folders to room names using 70+ patterns — no API calls. Analyzes directory structure to propose wing/room organization. Interactive approval flow for detected structure.

### Key functions to port
- detect_rooms_from_folders(path) → Vec<RoomMapping> — folder-based detection
- detect_rooms_from_files(path) → Vec<RoomMapping> — file content-based detection
- print_proposed_structure(mappings) — display proposed layout
- get_user_approval(mappings) → approved mappings

- detect_rooms_local(path) — main entry point

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- yaml → serde_yaml
- pathlib → std::path, walkdir
- collections → std::collections

### Rust considerations
- Pattern matching on path components — straightforward string ops
- 70+ patterns can be a const array of (pattern, room_name) tuples
- Interactive approval needs stdin reading (dialoguer crate or manual)
- YAML output via serde_yaml

### Acceptance criteria
- Detects common project structures (src/, tests/, docs/, etc.)
- Maps to meaningful room names
- Proposed structure is human-readable
- Config saved as valid YAML
- 70+ patterns all ported

### Test obligations
- Unit test: folder pattern matching
- Unit test: file-based detection
- Unit test: YAML output format
- Test with various project structures (Rust, Python, JS, mixed)

title: Port entity_detector.py → src/entity_detector.rs
description:
## Port: entity_detector.py (854 lines) → src/entity_detector.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/entity_detector.py

### What it does
Auto-detect people and projects from file content using heuristics (no LLM). Two-pass approach: extract candidates → score/classify. Uses signal patterns (person verbs, project verbs, dialogue markers) to distinguish entity types.

### Key functions to port
- extract_candidates(text) → Vec<Candidate> — find potential entities
- score_entity(candidate, context) → f64 — confidence score
- classify_entity(candidate) → EntityType (Person/Project/Unknown)
- detect_entities(text) → Vec<DetectedEntity> — main entry
- confirm_entities(detected, registry) — merge into registry
- scan_for_detection(paths) → scan multiple files

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- re → regex crate
- pathlib → std::path
- collections → std::collections

### Rust considerations
- Heavy regex usage — compile patterns once with lazy_static/once_cell
- Signal patterns are string lists — can be const arrays
- Scoring is arithmetic on pattern match counts
- Second-largest module (854 lines) — consider submodules

### Acceptance criteria
- Detects person names from dialogue patterns
- Detects project names from technical context
- Scoring ranks high-confidence entities first
- Classification distinguishes people from projects
- confirm_entities merges into registry correctly

### Test obligations
- Unit test: extract_candidates from sample text
- Unit test: scoring with known signals
- Unit test: classification accuracy
- Unit test: scan_for_detection with temp files
- Test with real conversation excerpts

title: MCP server: production-quality Rust MCP implementation (/mcp-builder)
description:
## MCP Server: Production-Quality Rust Implementation

### Source: /mcp-builder skill best practices

### What it does
Build the MCP server (src/mcp_server.rs) following MCP best practices from the mcp-builder skill. This bead covers the quality and architecture aspects beyond the basic port.

### MCP Best Practices to Follow

#### Tool Design
- Clear, descriptive tool names with consistent prefix: mempalace_search, mempalace_kg_query, etc.
- Concise tool descriptions with parameter docs and return type schemas
- Input validation with Zod-equivalent (serde + validation crate)
- Output schemas defined for structured data (not just text)
- Actionable error messages that guide agents toward solutions

#### Tool Annotations (per MCP spec)
Every tool must declare:
- readOnlyHint: true/false (search=true, add_drawer=false)
- destructiveHint: true/false (delete_drawer=true, search=false)
- idempotentHint: true/false (upsert=true, add=false)
- openWorldHint: true/false

#### Transport
- stdio transport for local usage (primary)
- Consider streamable HTTP for remote/multi-agent scenarios
- Stateless JSON for scalability

#### Consolidated Tool Set (14 tools per PR #25)
Read tools: status, list_wings, list_rooms, get_taxonomy, search, check_duplicate, traverse, find_tunnels, graph_stats, kg_query, kg_timeline, kg_stats, diary_read
Write tools: add_drawer, delete_drawer, kg_add, kg_invalidate, diary_write

#### Response Format
- JSON for structured data (search results, stats)
- Markdown for human-readable output (status, help)
- Pagination support for large result sets
- Concise responses — no unnecessary verbosity

#### Error Handling
- Typed error responses per MCP spec
- Actionable suggestions in error messages
- No internal path/data leaks in errors (security bead mr-2ce)

#### Read-Only Mode (PR #34)
- Separate read and write tool registration
- MEMPALACE_READONLY env var to disable write tools
- Useful for shared/public palace access

### Rust Implementation
- Use rmcp crate or implement JSON-RPC over stdio
- Each tool as an async handler function
- Typed input/output with serde
- Tower-based service architecture if using HTTP transport
- tracing for structured logging

### Acceptance criteria
- All 14+ tools registered with proper annotations
- Input validation on all parameters
- Structured output schemas defined
- Error messages are actionable
- Read-only mode works
- stdio transport works with Claude Code
- Tool descriptions are agent-friendly

### Test obligations
- Unit test per tool handler
- Integration test: full MCP initialize + tool call cycle
- Test error responses
- Test read-only mode
- Evaluation: 10 complex questions per mcp-builder Phase 4

title: install.sh: auto-install MCP config into user's providers
description:
## Feature: Auto-detect and auto-install MCP server config into user's AI tool providers

### Reference implementation
File: /home/quangdang/projects/aw/agents-auth/install.sh (lines 82-266)

### What it does
After building/installing the mempalace binary, automatically detect which AI coding tools the user has installed and inject the MCP server config into each one. Zero manual config editing required.

### Supported providers (from agents-auth reference)
| Provider | Config path | Config key | Format |
|----------|------------|------------|--------|
| Claude Code | ~/.claude.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| Codex | ~/.codex/config.toml | mcp_servers | TOML |
| Cursor | ~/.cursor/mcp.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| Windsurf | ~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| VS Code | .vscode/mcp.json | servers | JSON |
| Gemini | ~/.gemini/settings.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| OpenCode | ~/.opencode.json | mcpServers | JSON |
| Amp | ~/.config/amp/settings.json | amp.mcpServers | JSON |
| Droid | ~/.factory/mcp.json | mcpServers | JSON |

### Detection logic (from reference)
```
detect_hosts():
  ~/.codex/ exists → codex
  ~/.claude.json exists → claude-code
  ~/.cursor/ exists → cursor
  ~/.codeium/windsurf/ exists → windsurf
  .vscode/ exists → vscode
  ~/.gemini/ exists → gemini
  ~/.opencode.json exists → opencode
  ~/.config/amp/ exists → amp
  ~/.factory/ exists → droid
```

### Config injection pattern (from reference)
For JSON configs: read existing file → navigate to servers key → upsert "mempalace" entry → write back. Entry format:
```json
{"command": "/path/to/mempalace", "args": ["mcp"]}
```

For TOML configs (Codex): inject [mcp_servers.mempalace] section with command and args.

Status tracking: each install reports "installed" (new), "updated" (changed), or "unchanged".

### Rust implementation
Two places this runs:
1. install.sh — bash script during curl-pipe install (reference pattern already works)
2. mempalace install-mcp CLI subcommand — Rust native for post-install or manual trigger

For the Rust CLI subcommand:
- detect_providers() → Vec<Provider> — check filesystem for known paths
- install_mcp_config(provider, binary_path) → InstallResult — upsert config
- Use serde_json for JSON configs, toml crate for TOML configs
- Atomic writes (write to .tmp then rename)
- Preserve existing config (dont clobber user settings)
- Report per-provider status

For install.sh:
- Port the write_json_config() and write_toml_config() Python helpers to pure bash or keep Python (reference uses embedded Python)
- detect_hosts() is already pure bash
- Add mempalace-specific entry format

### CLI interface
```
mempalace install-mcp          # auto-detect and install all
mempalace install-mcp --host claude-code  # specific provider
mempalace install-mcp --list   # show detected providers
mempalace install-mcp --dry-run  # show what would change
mempalace uninstall-mcp        # remove from all providers
```

### Acceptance criteria
- Detects all 9 providers correctly
- JSON config injection preserves existing settings
- TOML config injection preserves existing settings
- Idempotent: running twice produces "unchanged"
- --dry-run shows changes without writing
- --list shows detected providers
- uninstall removes mempalace entries cleanly
- install.sh auto-runs detection after binary install
- Works on Linux, macOS, Windows (Git Bash)

### Test obligations
- Unit test: detect_providers with mock filesystem
- Unit test: JSON config injection (new file, existing file, existing entry)
- Unit test: TOML config injection
- Unit test: idempotency (install twice = unchanged)
- Unit test: uninstall removes entry
- Integration test: full install-mcp flow with temp home dir

title: refactor: unify onboarding + non-interactive init (PR #18 + PR #13)
description:
## Refactor: Unify onboarding and non-interactive init

### Source: PR #18 + PR #13 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
PR #18: Shared prompt helpers for interactive and non-interactive CLI flows. Onboarding, entity confirmation, and room approval use same safe prompt behavior.
PR #13: Handle non-interactive init gracefully — fall back safely when stdin unavailable (EOFError prevention).

### Rust implementation
Build this into onboarding.rs from the start:
- Use dialoguer crate with non-interactive fallback
- Check isatty(stdin) or MEMPALACE_NONINTERACTIVE env var
- Safe defaults when no TTY available
- Shared prompt helper functions

### Acceptance criteria
- Interactive mode works with dialoguer
- Non-interactive mode uses safe defaults
- No panic on EOF/closed stdin
- Works with piped input

### Test obligations
- Unit test: non-interactive fallback
- Unit test: safe defaults applied

title: Port layers.py → src/layers.rs
description:
## Port: layers.py (507 lines) → src/layers.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/layers.py

### What it does
Implements the 4-layer memory stack: L0 (identity, ~100 tokens), L1 (essential story, ~500-800 tokens), L2 (on-demand wing/room filtered, ~200-500 tokens), L3 (deep search, unlimited). MemoryStack orchestrates all layers.

### Key structures to port
- Layer0 struct — loads identity from config
- Layer1 struct — top palace moments (pre-computed)
- Layer2 struct — wing/room filtered retrieval
- Layer3 struct — full semantic search
- MemoryStack struct — orchestrates all layers
- Methods: wake_up(), recall(), search(), status()

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- chromadb → vector DB client
- pathlib → std::path
- collections → std::collections
- config module (internal)

### Rust considerations
- Trait-based layer design: trait MemoryLayer { fn recall(&self, query) -> Vec<Memory> }
- MemoryStack holds Vec<Box<dyn MemoryLayer>> or concrete fields
- Token counting approximation (chars/4 or use tiktoken-rs)
- wake_up() composes L0+L1 into a prompt string

### Acceptance criteria
- L0 returns identity text within token budget
- L1 returns top moments within token budget
- L2 filters by wing/room correctly
- L3 performs full semantic search
- wake_up() produces combined context string
- status() reports per-layer statistics

### Test obligations
- Unit test per layer
- Integration test: wake_up with populated palace
- Test token budget enforcement
- Test empty palace handling

title: perf: batch I/O and hash-set dedup in mining (PR #38)
description:
## Perf: Algorithmic improvements across hot paths

### Source: PR #38 (milla-jovovich/mempalace)

### What it does
Two key performance improvements:
1. Batch I/O: accumulate chunks and insert in single batch call per file (was 1 call per chunk)
2. Hash-set dedup: pre-fetch all known source_files in one query, check membership in HashSet (was 1 query per file)

### Rust implementation
Build these optimizations into miner.rs and convo_miner.rs from the start:
- Collect all chunks for a file, then batch-insert via single vector DB call
- Pre-fetch known source files into HashSet<String> before scanning
- Use rayon for parallel file processing if beneficial

### Acceptance criteria
- Mining 500 files uses O(1) dedup queries, not O(n)
- Chunks batch-inserted per file, not per chunk
- Performance measurably better than naive approach

### Test obligations
- Benchmark: mine N files, measure DB calls
- Unit test: batch insert produces same results as individual inserts

title: Port general_extractor.py → src/general_extractor.rs
description:
## Port: general_extractor.py (522 lines) → src/general_extractor.rs

### Python reference
File: references/mempalace-main/mempalace/general_extractor.py

### What it does
Extracts 5 memory types from text using keyword/pattern heuristics (no LLM): DECISIONS, PREFERENCES, MILESTONES, PROBLEMS, EMOTIONAL. Segments text, scores markers, disambiguates overlapping types, detects sentiment and resolution.

### Key functions to port
- extract_memories(text) → Vec<Memory> — main entry
- _split_into_segments(text) — break text into logical segments
- _split_by_turns(text) — split by speaker turns
- _score_markers(segment) → HashMap<MemoryType, f64> — keyword scoring
- _disambiguate(scores) → MemoryType — pick best type
- _get_sentiment(text) → Sentiment — positive/negative/neutral
- _has_resolution(text) → bool — check if problem was resolved

### Dependencies (Python → Rust)
- re → regex crate
- No external dependencies — pure heuristics

### Rust considerations
- MemoryType as enum with 5 variants
- Marker patterns as const arrays of &str
- Scoring is keyword counting + weighting
- Sentiment detection via positive/negative word lists
- Pure functions — easy to test

### Acceptance criteria
- All 5 memory types detected correctly
- Segmentation handles multi-turn conversations
- Disambiguation picks strongest signal
- Sentiment detection works
- Resolution detection works for problems

### Test obligations
- Unit test per memory type with sample text
- Unit test: segmentation
- Unit test: disambiguation with competing signals
- Unit test: sentiment detection
- Unit test: resolution detection
